content.problemStatement:
Chef has a binary string SSS of length NNN.

Chef can perform the following operation on the string:


Choose any substring of SSS;
Remove the chosen substring from SSS;
Concatenate the remaining parts of the string SSS, obtained after removing the substring.
Find the length of the longest prefix having **all** 111s you can achieve, by performing the above operation **at most** once.

Note: A prefix of a string is obtained by deleting some (possibly zero) characters from the end of string SSS.


Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of multiple lines of input.

The first line of each test case contains a single integer NNN, the length of the binary string.
The next line contains the binary string SSS.




Output Format
For each test case, print in a new line, the length of the longest prefix having **all** 111s you can achieve, by performing the operation **at most** once.
constraints: The sum of N over all test cases does not exceed 2*10^5
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. For each test case: the first line contains an integer N, the length of the binary string. The second line contains the binary string S of length N consisting only of characters '0' and '1'.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line containing the maximum possible length of a prefix consisting only of '1's after performing at most one substring removal operation.

sampleTestCases.explanation: For each test case we keep the leading ones, then add the longest block of ones that appears after the first zero. The computed lengths are 2, 2 and 3 respectively.
First string is already all ones, so answer is 5. Second string has no ones, so even after removal the longest prefix of ones is length 0.
Leading ones = 3. After the first zero the longest block of consecutive ones is of length 4. 3+4=7.

hidden.label: edge case with leading ones then zeros then long ones

solution.approach: Let cnt1 be the number of consecutive '1's from the beginning of S. These leading ones can never be harmed because removing any character before them would only shorten the prefix. The first zero appears at position cnt1 (if it exists). By deleting a substring that starts at this first zero and ends just before some later position k, we can bring the block of ones that starts at k to the front, directly after the kept leading ones. Therefore the best we can achieve is cnt1 plus the length of the longest contiguous block of '1's that starts somewhere after the first zero. We obtain this by a single linear scan: after counting the leading ones, scan the rest of the string, keep the length of the current run of '1's, and track the maximum run length. The answer is cnt1 + maxRun. If the whole string is already all '1's, the answer is N.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N) per test case, where N is the length of the string. The total over all test cases is O( Σ N ) ≤ O(2·10^5).
